narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 51% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. This prediction is based on your profile: a Year 1 school decile rank of 5, Year 3 rank of 4, middle family income bracket, LSAT score of 27, strong undergraduate GPA of 3.80, female gender, White racial background, and full-time enrollment plans. While your undergrad GPA stands out as higher than the typical 3.21 average, your LSAT is notably lower than the usual 36.7, and your school deciles are around or slightly below the dataset averages of 5.71 and 5.53. The model's decision was influenced most strongly by your Year 3 decile rank, which had the biggest impact and nudged the prediction toward expecting a bar failure since it's in the lower-middle range compared to peers. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile rank, also pushing slightly toward failure relative to typical students. Your middle family income followed closely, contributing a small push in the same direction, as did your LSAT score, which carried moderate weight given how far below average it is. Other factors like your racial background, undergrad GPA, full-time status, and gender had progressively smaller influences, all leaning slightly toward the failure prediction but not enough to outweigh the top ones on their own. Overall, no single factor doomed your chances—it was the combined subtle effects, led by those early law school performance indicators, that tipped the scale to 51%. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile is solid in undergrad performance and demographics but lags in LSAT and school rankings, where stronger showings correlate with higher pass rates. To boost your future odds, focus on excelling in law school coursework right away to climb those decile ranks, as they proved most predictive here. Retaking the LSAT for a higher score could help if you reapply, since it still mattered. Building dedicated bar exam prep habits early, perhaps through targeted study or tutoring, would address the model's core concern about first-try success. With your strong GPA as a foundation, these steps could realistically shift your predicted probability well below 50% next time. Keep pushing—you have the potential to outperform this prediction.
